Notes:
1. We observed a fasting day every Monday my mother called Sombar brata.
2. My mother fasted strictly — only water until evening.
3. She said it was for my father's long life and good health.
4. My father did not fast himself but he never ate until she had broken her fast.
5. Neither of them spoke about this arrangement — it was simply how things were.

Every Monday my mother fasted — from morning until evening, only water — and she did it for my father's health and long life, she said, because that was the meaning of Sombar brata. My father did not fast with her, but I noticed over the years that he never sat down to eat until she had finished her prayers and broken hers. No one announced this arrangement and no one discussed it. It was simply how Monday evenings worked in our house, understood by everyone and explained by no one. I think that is what a long marriage sometimes looks like from the outside: not words, but the timing of meals.

Notes:
1. My father's best friend was a man named Dhan Bahadur.
2. The two men had known each other since childhood.
3. They argued constantly but never fell out permanently.
4. When my father died, Dhan Bahadur sat outside the house all day without being asked.
5. He did not come inside. He just sat there, which was enough.

My father's oldest friend was Dhan Bahadur, and the two of them had known each other since they were boys. They argued with a frequency and a familiarity that looked, from the outside, exactly like closeness — which I suppose is what it was. I never saw them fall out permanently. When my father died, Dhan Bahadur came and sat outside the house. He did not come inside. He did not offer condolences or explanations. He sat on the step from morning until late afternoon and then he went home. I do not know what he needed from those hours, or what he was doing. But I know that his being there mattered, and that some presences communicate everything precisely through what they do not say.

Notes:
1. My eldest granddaughter Priya was always full of questions.
2. She carried a notebook from the time she was seven.
3. She was the one who asked me to tell my stories.
4. She listened with her whole body, leaning forward.
5. Because of her, these stories exist.

My eldest granddaughter Priya carried a notebook from the time she was seven years old, and she filled them with things she noticed and questions she had not yet found answers to. She was the one who came to me and asked me to tell my stories — not once, but persistently, in the way she pursued everything that interested her. When I spoke she leaned forward, her whole body listening, and this made me want to remember better than I thought I could. I do not know what she will do with all of it, or what these stories will become in her hands. But I know that without her asking, most of them would have stayed inside me and gone nowhere. That is no small thing to have done for a family.

Notes:
1. [Place] The flat rock by the stream was where we gathered after school.
2. [Person] My best friend Laxmi told me there that she was moving to Pokhara.
3. [Emotion] I did not understand at the time that friendships could simply end.
4. [Time] We were around twelve.
5. [Festival] It was a few days after Tihar — the decorations were still up in the lanes.

A few days after Tihar, when the decorations were still up in the lanes and the year felt like it had just turned something over, Laxmi and I were sitting on the flat rock by the stream where we always went after school, and she told me her family was moving to Pokhara. We were around twelve, and I did not fully understand what the announcement meant — that some friendships do not survive distance at that age, that this might be a kind of ending I had no category for yet. We went back to school the next morning and the one after that, and then she was gone. The rock was still there, of course. I went back to it a few times alone and then stopped. Some places belong to a particular arrangement of people, and when the arrangement changes, the place is still there but it is a different place.

Notes:
1. Losar was celebrated by our Tamang neighbors and we were sometimes invited.
2. The food was different from anything we ate at home — momos and butter tea.
3. The family sang together in a way our family did not.
4. I found butter tea very strange at first.
5. By the third or fourth time I had learned to love it.

Our Tamang neighbors observed Losar every year, and sometimes they invited us. Everything about the celebration was different from what I knew at home — the food was momos and butter tea instead of what we usually ate, and the family sang together in a natural, comfortable way that I did not see in my own house. The butter tea was strange to me the first time, a taste I did not understand, and I drank it carefully so as not to seem rude. But they invited us back the following year, and the year after that, and by the third or fourth time I had learned to love it. Belonging to a neighborhood means learning what the other houses carry.

Notes:
1. My grandmother Hajuramma told stories every night after dinner.
2. She knew hundreds of stories and never repeated one in the same way twice.
3. She was blind in one eye from childhood but never let it slow her.
4. She outlived two of her children and never spoke about her grief.
5. She always said: worry is a debt you pay on a loan you may never receive.

My grandmother — we all called her Hajuramma — told stories every night after dinner, and I never once heard her tell the same one in exactly the same way twice. She had been blind in one eye since she was a girl, and she moved through the world with such complete indifference to this that for years I forgot it was true. She outlived two of her children, which is a thing no parent should have to do, and she did not speak about those losses in the way others spoke about losses — she carried them without display. She used to say that worry was a debt you paid on a loan you might never receive. She was not a woman who paid unnecessary debts.

Notes:
1. My grandmother kept her own small garden of herbs behind the house.
2. She knew which herb was for which ailment.
3. Neighbors came to her when they were sick and she never turned anyone away.
4. She had no formal training — her knowledge came from her own mother.
5. When she died, a kind of medicine died with her.

My grandmother kept a small herb garden behind the house that she tended entirely alone, and she carried in her memory a knowledge of what each plant could do that no one had written down anywhere. When neighbors were ill they came to her, and she never turned anyone away — she would go into the garden and return with something, and she would explain what to do with it in terms so practical and certain that you believed her before you had taken it. This knowledge came from her own mother and from no formal source. When she died, it ended. I have thought about that many times since — how much a single person can hold, and how completely it can disappear, and how you do not fully understand the size of the loss until long after there is any way to prevent it.
